4.6.a.info:
ip address dhcp is an interface-level command that turns the interface into a DHCP client. Instead of hardcoding an IP with ip address 192.0.2.1 255.255.255.0, the router sends a DORA (Discover / Offer / Request / Ack) sequence out this interface and installs whatever IP, mask, and optional default gateway the server hands back.

Behind the scenes, once you issue no shutdown, the interface is in up/up state but carries no IPv4 address until the DHCP server responds. Cisco IOS then installs the leased address and creates a connected route plus a local (/32) route in the routing table, exactly like a static interface would. If the server also sends a default gateway option, IOS installs a static default route with the next-hop pointing at that gateway.

The classic use case is a WAN edge router where the ISP controls addressing (residential cable/DSL, some business fiber). It is less common on LAN interfaces because a router usually wants a stable static IP that clients use as a default-gateway. Putting ip address dhcp on a LAN interface means the gateway IP could change on every reboot, which breaks the DHCP scope the router itself serves.

Verification commands: show ip interface brief shows the learned IP instead of 'unassigned'; show dhcp lease shows lease time, server IP, and options received. You can release and renew with release dhcp [interface] / renew dhcp [interface] in privileged EXEC.

Common mistake: confusing this with the router acting as a DHCP server. ip address dhcp makes the router a CLIENT only — it receives an IP. Serving IPs to other devices requires an ip dhcp pool.

hack.memory: 'ip address dhcp' = 'I don't have an address, please assign one.' The router becomes a DHCP client. Almost always lives on a WAN/ISP-facing interface, never on the LAN side where you need a stable gateway IP.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: put a DHCP server on one side of a router. Configure the router's outside interface with 'ip address dhcp' + 'no shutdown'. Run 'show ip interface brief' — the IP appears after a few seconds. Run 'show dhcp lease' and note the server IP and lease time. Release and renew to watch DORA fire again.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 (DHCP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. The exam asks 'which command configures an interface to receive its IP via DHCP?' — answer is 'ip address dhcp'. Remember this makes the router a CLIENT, not a server.

4.6.a.info:
A Cisco router acting as a DHCP client is most common on a WAN uplink where the service provider owns the addressing. Cable, DSL, and many metro-Ethernet services hand out public IPs via DHCP. Putting ip address dhcp on the outside interface lets the router bring up the link without manual coordination every time the ISP reassigns an IP.

The DORA exchange is the same one a PC uses. The router sends a DHCPDISCOVER out the configured interface (broadcast destination 255.255.255.255 / FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). Any DHCP server on that segment that has a matching pool responds with a DHCPOFFER. The router replies with a DHCPREQUEST selecting one offer, and the server finalizes with a DHCPACK that carries the lease time, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS servers.

Key options the router can consume: Option 3 (router / default gateway — IOS installs this as a static default route via the DHCP-learned next hop), Option 6 (DNS servers — populated into the device's name-server list), Option 51 (lease time — controls when the router must renew), and Option 15 (domain name).

To prevent the DHCP-learned default route from fighting with a manually configured one, IOS installs it with a specific administrative distance. If you also have a static default route, you can control preference with floating-static AD values.

Troubleshooting flow: (1) verify L1/L2 up with show interfaces; (2) verify no IP learned with show ip interface brief; (3) debug with debug dhcp detail to see Discover/Offer/Request/Ack exchanges. No offer usually means no reachable DHCP server or a broadcast path problem.

hack.memory: Router-as-DHCP-client = same DORA as a laptop, just sourced from a WAN interface. Router learns IP + gateway (Option 3) + DNS (Option 6) + lease (Option 51). If your outside interface stays 'unassigned' with 'ip address dhcp' configured, DORA is failing somewhere — check L1, then run 'debug dhcp detail'.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build ISP-router + your-router with a shared link. Put 'ip address dhcp' on your router's WAN. Confirm with 'show ip interface brief' and 'show ip route' — you should see a connected route from DHCP plus a static default route also from DHCP (0.0.0.0/0 via the learned gateway).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. The exam may describe an edge router that 'learns its IP from the provider' — that points directly at 'ip address dhcp'. Also know that the router can then redistribute that learned default route into routing protocols with 'default-information originate'.

4.6.b.info:
ip helper-address is the interface-level command that turns a Cisco router (or L3 switch SVI) into a DHCP relay agent. When a client sends a broadcast DHCPDISCOVER on the subnet, the relay catches the broadcast, rewrites it as a unicast to the server IP configured in the helper statement, and forwards it across the routed network.

Syntax: interface Gi0/0 → ip helper-address 10.50.50.10. The IP you list is the DHCP server's IP, not the client's. You can stack multiple helper addresses on the same interface to send the relayed request to several servers in parallel — useful when you have redundant DHCP servers in different data centers.

When the relay forwards the message, it fills in the GIADDR (Gateway IP Address) field of the DHCP header with the IP of the interface that received the broadcast. The server uses GIADDR to pick the correct pool (a pool whose network statement matches the GIADDR subnet). Without a valid GIADDR, the server has no idea which subnet the client is on.

Helper-address does not only forward DHCP. By default it also relays seven UDP services at once: BOOTP 67/68, TFTP 69, DNS 53, NetBIOS name service 137, NetBIOS datagram service 138, TACACS 49, and Time 37. You can add or remove ports with global ip forward-protocol udp [port] and no ip forward-protocol udp [port].

Verification: show ip interface Gi0/0 shows 'Helper address is 10.50.50.10' on the interface. Packet captures between the relay and server should show unicast DHCP messages sourced from the relay's IP to the server's IP.

hack.memory: 'ip helper-address <server-IP>' lives on the CLIENT-facing interface and points at the SERVER. It converts the client's DHCPDISCOVER broadcast into a unicast the server can actually see. Also quietly forwards BOOTP, TFTP, DNS, NetBIOS NS/DS, TACACS, and Time — not just DHCP.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer: put a DHCP server on VLAN 20, a PC on VLAN 10, and route between them. Without helper-address the PC can't DHCP. Add 'ip helper-address <server-IP>' on the VLAN 10 SVI. PC now gets an IP. Confirm on the server with 'show ip dhcp binding' — the GIADDR decides which pool was used.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 (DHCP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Heavy-hitter topic: 'where does ip helper-address go?' (client-facing interface), 'what IP does it point to?' (server), 'why is it needed?' (routers drop broadcasts). Also know the 7 default UDP ports.

4.6.b.info:
The #1 deployment error with DHCP relay is configuring ip helper-address on the wrong side of the router. It must go on the interface facing the clients — the same interface that the clients use as their default gateway — not the interface facing the DHCP server.

Why: the relay has to receive the client's broadcast before it can forward it. Broadcasts stay on the local segment. Only the interface connected to that segment sees them, so that interface is the one that needs the helper statement. Configuring helper-address on the server-facing interface means the broadcast is already lost before the relay ever sees it.

In a typical campus design with inter-VLAN routing, helper-address is configured on each SVI (interface vlan X) that represents a client VLAN. VLAN 10 SVI gets a helper, VLAN 20 SVI gets a helper, and so on. Each SVI becomes its own relay for its subnet, and each uses its own IP as GIADDR so the server knows which pool to pull from.

On routers with many client subnets (branch offices, large access switches), you usually repeat the same helper statement on every client-facing SVI. Some shops template this into configuration standards so it's always consistent.

Exam framing: scenario describes clients failing to get an IP, then shows a config with ip helper-address on the wrong interface. The fix is always 'move it to the client-facing interface'. Also common: asking which interface on a multi-VLAN L3 switch needs the helper — answer is each client SVI.

hack.memory: Helper-address goes on the CLIENT'S gateway interface. You only catch broadcasts on the segment they're broadcast on. Put it on the server side and it never hears a peep from the client.
hack.practice: Build a topology with two client VLANs (10 and 20) and one DHCP server on VLAN 99. Put helper-address on VLAN 10 SVI only. VLAN 10 PCs get IPs; VLAN 20 PCs fail. Add helper-address to VLAN 20 SVI and watch VLAN 20 start working. This one lab wires in the concept for good.
hack.meta: Exam trap: config screenshot shows helper-address on the server-side interface; question asks why DHCP fails. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8.

4.6.b.info:
The IP listed after ip helper-address is the DHCP server's IP address. It is the destination the relay unicasts the converted DHCP message to. A common rookie mistake is entering the client's subnet or the relay's own IP — both will break relaying.

The server IP can live anywhere the router has a route to — same building, different floor, different data center. As long as a routed path exists between the relay interface and the server IP, the unicast DHCP will reach it. The source IP of that unicast is the relay's interface IP (which is also what lands in GIADDR), so the server's reply is routed back to the relay naturally.

You can also configure multiple helper statements on a single interface for redundancy. Example: ip helper-address 10.50.50.10 + ip helper-address 10.50.50.11. The relay sends the request to BOTH servers simultaneously. Whichever answers first gets used; duplicate offers are handled by the client selecting one in the DHCPREQUEST stage.

Design consideration: if you're relaying to a cluster of DHCP servers (Microsoft DHCP failover, ISC Kea pairs, Infoblox appliances), list all of them. It's cheap insurance. Servers behind a load balancer or VIP can be represented by a single helper address pointing at the VIP, but classic DHCP has some quirks with L7 load balancers — direct unicast to each server is simpler.

Verification: show ip interface [name] lists every helper address. show running-config interface [name] shows them in config form. Packet capture on the relay-to-server path should show UDP 67 unicast traffic sourced from the relay, destined to each listed helper IP.

hack.memory: Helper points at the SERVER's IP — the unicast destination. Stack multiple 'ip helper-address' lines for redundant servers; the relay will send to all of them. Source IP of the relayed DHCP = the relay's interface IP = the GIADDR.
hack.practice: Take your single-helper lab and add a second DHCP server. Configure TWO 'ip helper-address' lines on the client SVI. Capture on the server-side — both servers see the DISCOVER. Pull the power on the primary server and verify clients still get a lease from the backup.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam: 'which IP do you enter in ip helper-address?' = the DHCP server's IP. Multiple helpers are allowed for redundancy.

4.6.c.info:
The reason DHCP relay even exists is a fundamental routing rule: routers do not forward Layer 2 broadcasts between interfaces. Each router interface is its own broadcast domain. A frame with destination MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF or an IP of 255.255.255.255 reaches the router and stops there.

DHCPDISCOVER is a broadcast. The client has no IP yet, doesn't know a default gateway, and doesn't know where the DHCP server is, so it blasts the Discover as a local broadcast and hopes someone is listening. On the client's local subnet, any DHCP server (or relay agent) will hear it.

If the DHCP server lives on a different subnet, the router between them drops the broadcast at L3. The server never sees the Discover, the client never gets an Offer, and the client eventually self-assigns an APIPA 169.254.x.x address. That APIPA address is the telltale sign that DHCP failed to reach a server.

This isn't a bug — it's a design feature. Without broadcast containment, every DHCP Discover, ARP request, and NetBIOS lookup would flood the entire enterprise network, and broadcast storms would take down large segments. Broadcasts are intentionally contained to small fault domains.

The fix is DHCP relay. The relay agent listens for broadcasts on the client subnet, converts them to unicasts pointed at a known DHCP server IP, and forwards them across the routed network. The server's response comes back the same way — unicast to the relay, then broadcast (or unicast) to the client depending on the BROADCAST flag in the DHCP header.

hack.memory: Routers = broadcast firewalls. DHCPDISCOVER is a broadcast. Broadcasts die at every router interface. No relay = no DHCP across subnets = APIPA 169.254.x.x on the client.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer simulation mode: client sends DHCPDISCOVER with no 'ip helper-address' configured. Watch the frame hit the router and vanish. Check the client — it ends up with APIPA. Add 'ip helper-address' and re-run; now the frame survives as a unicast past the router.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 and Day 6 (broadcast domains). Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 5 and 8. Exam wording: 'why can a client on VLAN 10 not get an address from a DHCP server on VLAN 20 without configuration?' — routers block broadcasts; need a relay.

4.6.c.info:
A DHCP relay agent is any device (usually a router or L3 switch) that forwards DHCP messages between clients on one subnet and servers on another. On Cisco, it's activated with ip helper-address. On Linux/Unix, with dhcrelay. On enterprise firewalls, often a checkbox in the DHCP settings.

Functionally, the relay does three things: (1) receives the client's broadcast DHCP message on the client-facing interface; (2) rewrites it as a unicast IP packet destined to the configured DHCP server, setting GIADDR = the relay interface's IP so the server knows which subnet to serve from; (3) forwards the server's reply back toward the client, either as a unicast to the client's new IP or a broadcast onto the client's subnet, controlled by the BROADCAST flag.

Relays make centralized DHCP possible. Without them, every subnet would need its own DHCP server — operationally painful at scale. With relays, a single DHCP server (or pair) at HQ serves every subnet in every branch, each subnet's relay pointing at HQ's DHCP IP.

Relay vs server: a relay does not hold the pool. It doesn't decide what IP to hand out. It just shuttles messages between the client and the real DHCP server. Config on the relay is trivial — one line per interface. The intelligence lives on the server.

Relay is also what makes features like Option 82 (DHCP relay agent information) work — the relay can insert information like circuit ID and remote ID so the server can apply per-port or per-customer policies. This is used heavily in ISP edge deployments and port-based authentication.

hack.memory: Relay agent = postal courier. Takes the client's broadcast envelope, stuffs it in a unicast envelope addressed to the DHCP server, drops it on the routed network, then hands the server's reply back to the client. Relay has no pool, makes no decisions — it just shuttles messages.
hack.practice: In a lab with 3 VLANs + 1 central DHCP server, configure helper-address on all 3 VLAN SVIs pointing at the server. Watch every PC in every VLAN get an IP from the matching pool (server uses GIADDR to pick pools). This is exactly how enterprise centralized DHCP works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Know: relay forwards broadcast as unicast, uses GIADDR, enables centralized DHCP, and is configured with 'ip helper-address' on the client interface.

4.6.c.info:
Centralized DHCP means one (or a small cluster of) DHCP server(s) hand out addresses for every subnet in the enterprise — instead of a local DHCP server per site or per VLAN. It's the dominant design in medium/large networks because it reduces operational overhead.

Benefits: one place to audit leases, one place to update scopes, one place to patch. Integrating with IPAM (Infoblox, SolarWinds, Bluecat) is straightforward with centralized servers. Option 82 policies for per-port security are easier to manage. Backup and DR are simpler.

Architecture: DHCP server lives in a data center or secure management VLAN. Every client-facing SVI in the campus has ip helper-address pointing at that server (or a VIP for a server cluster). The server's scopes are defined per subnet, matching the GIADDR inserted by each relay.

Scope selection on the server: when GIADDR = 10.10.10.1 (relay interface IP on VLAN 10), the server looks for a scope whose network matches 10.10.10.0/24 and pulls an IP from there. When GIADDR = 10.20.20.1, it picks from the 10.20.20.0/24 scope. The relay's interface IP is the critical hint that makes centralized DHCP work.

Redundancy: pair two DHCP servers with failover (Windows DHCP Failover protocol, ISC Kea HA, or Active/Passive cluster). List both server IPs on every relay interface (ip helper-address x2). Most enterprises also keep lease times short enough (4-12 hours) that a failed server won't cause widespread outages.

children.name: Site A — VLAN 10 SVI helper → HQ
Site B — VLAN 20 SVI helper → HQ
Site C — VLAN 30 SVI helper → HQ

hack.memory: Centralized DHCP = one DHCP server rules all subnets. Every client-facing SVI has 'ip helper-address' pointing at the central server. GIADDR tells the server which scope to pull from. Add a second helper-address for redundancy.
hack.practice: Design exercise: sketch a 3-site network with 2 VLANs per site. Mark the DHCP server at HQ. Draw an arrow from each client SVI to the DHCP server's IP labeled 'helper-address'. This visualization makes the centralized pattern click.
hack.meta: Less commonly drilled on the exam but conceptually important. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Know that centralized DHCP + relay is the standard enterprise design.

4.6.d.info:
Cisco IOS can act as a full DHCP server. The complete recipe has six commands executed in order: (1) exclude static IPs, (2) create the pool, (3) define the network, (4) set the default router, (5) set the DNS server, (6) set the lease time.

Example for a /24 subnet:

! 1) Exclude static IPs (GLOBAL config, before the pool)
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10
!
! 2) Create the pool
ip dhcp pool LAN
!
! 3) Network / subnet
 network 10.1.1.0 /24
!
! 4) Default gateway (the router's LAN IP)
 default-router 10.1.1.1
!
! 5) DNS servers
 dns-server 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
!
! 6) Lease time (days hours minutes)
 lease 7

Critical trap: the command is default-router, NOT default-gateway. 'default-gateway' exists but it's a device-level command for a switch or router without routing enabled, not a DHCP pool option. Many students lose points swapping these.

Optional extras inside the pool: domain-name example.com, netbios-name-server x.x.x.x, option 150 ip x.x.x.x (used for Cisco IP phones to find their TFTP server), import all (import DHCP options from an upstream DHCP server, useful when the Cisco router is both a client and a relay).

Verification: show ip dhcp pool (pool utilization), show ip dhcp binding (active leases), show running-config | section dhcp (full DHCP config).

hack.memory: 6-step recipe: EXCLUDE → POOL → NETWORK → DEFAULT-ROUTER → DNS-SERVER → LEASE. Mnemonic: 'Every Pool Needs Default DNS Leased' (E-P-N-D-D-L). TRAP: it's 'default-ROUTER' not 'default-gateway' inside the pool.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build the entire pool from memory. Verify with 'show ip dhcp pool' — should show total, leased, excluded counts. Connect 3 PCs, confirm each gets an IP, gateway, and DNS. Practice typing all 6 commands in under 60 seconds until it's muscle memory for the exam lab sim.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Lab-sim territory. 'default-router' vs 'default-gateway' is the most-missed trap. Exclusions go in GLOBAL config, the rest go inside the pool.

4.6.d.info:
Technically, Cisco IOS accepts the DHCP configuration commands in either order (exclusion before pool, or pool before exclusion). Practically, exclusions should be configured BEFORE clients start requesting IPs. If you create a pool first, let clients grab addresses, then add exclusions, the already-leased IPs stay assigned until the leases expire — the exclusion only prevents FUTURE assignments.

Best practice sequence: (1) ip dhcp excluded-address for every static IP on the subnet; (2) ip dhcp pool NAME; (3) all pool sub-commands. This order guarantees that no lease is ever issued from the excluded range.

If you need to retroactively exclude addresses after leases exist, pair the exclusion with clear ip dhcp binding * (clears all active bindings) or clear ip dhcp binding [ip] (clears a single binding). Clients renew and get safe addresses.

Inside the pool itself, most sub-commands are order-independent at save time — IOS reorders them in the running config. But when typing, follow the recipe order above so you don't forget any component.

One order that DOES matter: if you change the network statement, IOS may clear existing bindings for the old network and restart. Don't modify a production pool's network statement without planning a brief outage window.

hack.memory: Exclude BEFORE pool goes live. IOS will accept commands in any order, but clients grabbing .1 before you exclude it = active IP conflict. Always configure exclusions first, especially for the gateway IP.
hack.practice: Deliberately create a pool without exclusions, let a PC grab 10.1.1.1 (your planned gateway), then configure the gateway statically and watch the conflict. Fix with 'clear ip dhcp binding *' + add exclusion. This bad experience teaches the right order forever.
hack.meta: Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam may ask 'what happens if you add an exclusion after leases exist?' — existing leases stay until expiration; exclusion only affects new leases. Pair with 'clear ip dhcp binding' to force renewal.

4.6.d.info:
A Cisco IOS router can host a full DHCP server — not just relay. It supports multiple pools, per-pool options, address exclusions, conflict detection, and all the standard DHCP options (gateway, DNS, domain name, NTP, TFTP server, etc.). For small branch offices and home-office deployments, the router-as-server model eliminates the need for a separate DHCP appliance.

Typical deployment: branch router serves DHCP for the local LAN VLANs. One ip dhcp pool per subnet, each with its own network, default-router, DNS, lease, and options. The router also handles routing, NAT, and security for the branch — a single device for the whole site.

Limits: a Cisco router is fine for small-to-medium subnets (up to a few hundred clients per pool). For enterprise-scale DHCP (thousands of clients, option 82 policies, failover, IPAM integration), use dedicated DHCP servers (Windows, ISC Kea, Infoblox). The router's DHCP daemon is single-threaded and not optimized for huge volumes.

Important operational detail: the router-as-DHCP-server does NOT lease its own interface IPs. The router keeps a local /32 for each interface and will refuse to hand out an address that overlaps with a configured static IP. Conflict detection via ICMP ping before handing out a lease helps catch duplicates (ip dhcp ping packets 2 sends 2 pings, 500ms timeout default).

Combine with relay on other subnets: a single router can be the DHCP server for its own LAN and also a relay for DHCP traffic heading to another server elsewhere — different interfaces, different roles.

hack.memory: Cisco router = full DHCP server (multiple pools, full options, conflict detection). Perfect for small branches. Not the right tool for enterprise-scale DHCP — that's what dedicated servers are for. Router can also do DHCP AND relay simultaneously on different interfaces.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: give a single router 2 LAN VLANs with SVIs. Create 2 pools on the same router, each for its own VLAN. Connect PCs to each VLAN and verify each PC gets an IP from the correct pool. Then test multi-role: keep the same router serving DHCP on VLAN 10 while relaying to an external DHCP server for VLAN 20.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam: 'can a Cisco router serve as a DHCP server?' = yes. 'Can it do server AND relay on the same device?' = yes, on different interfaces.

4.6.e.info:
ip dhcp excluded-address is a global configuration command (NOT a sub-command of ip dhcp pool). It reserves specific IPs or ranges so the DHCP server never leases them to clients. The most common mistake is trying to enter it inside the pool — IOS will reject it.

Syntax:

! Single IP
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1
!
! Range
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10
!
! Multiple non-contiguous ranges
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.200 10.1.1.210

You can stack as many exclusion statements as you need. Each is evaluated independently when the DHCP server picks an address from the pool.

Typical things to exclude on any subnet: the router's LAN IP (default-gateway), statically assigned servers, printers, access points, VoIP gateways, management IPs, and network devices like switches with SVIs. A common convention is to exclude the bottom 10-20 addresses (e.g., .1 through .20) for static assignment and let DHCP start leasing from .21 onward.

When exclusions are configured, show ip dhcp pool shows the reduced pool size and the number of excluded addresses. This is a good sanity check before putting the pool into production.

Edge case: if you remove an exclusion with no ip dhcp excluded-address ..., those IPs become eligible for leasing immediately. Be careful — you could hand out an IP that a static device is still using.

hack.memory: 'ip dhcp excluded-address' lives in GLOBAL config, not inside the pool. Reserves IPs from leasing. Stack multiple lines for multiple ranges. Always exclude the gateway, at minimum.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure a pool WITHOUT exclusions, give the router 10.1.1.1 as its static gateway, and connect 5 PCs. Watch one PC end up with 10.1.1.1 → IP conflict. Fix by adding 'ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10' in global config, then 'clear ip dhcp binding *'. Re-check — PCs now lease from .11 onward.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam focus: (1) exclusions are GLOBAL config, not pool config; (2) must exclude statically assigned IPs; (3) syntax supports single or range.

4.6.e.info:
The router's own LAN interface IP is almost always the default-gateway for that subnet (commonly .1 or .254). If you don't exclude it, the DHCP server has no idea it's taken and can hand out a conflicting address to the next client that joins the network.

Impact of a gateway conflict: two devices with the same IP on the same subnet triggers ARP flapping — the MAC-address mapping for .1 in ARP tables bounces between the router's MAC and the PC's MAC. Half the traffic that should exit through the gateway gets black-holed. Users see intermittent internet outages. The root cause can be painful to diagnose without looking at ARP tables.

Rule: always exclude every statically assigned IP on the subnet. Gateway is the most obvious one, but don't forget servers, printers, APs, and anything else on a fixed address. A safe blanket rule is to exclude a range (like .1-.20) so you have room to add future static devices without remembering to update exclusions.

Some organizations standardize on patterns like: .1 for gateway, .2-.10 for infrastructure (switches, APs, printers), .11-.100 for DHCP, .101-.200 for servers with static IPs, .201-.254 reserved for future use. Exclusions mirror the static ranges: ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10 and ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.101 10.1.1.254.

If a conflict does occur despite exclusions (perhaps from an unauthorized static IP on the network), Cisco's conflict detection catches it: the DHCP server pings an address before leasing it. If it responds, the IP goes into the conflict list (visible with show ip dhcp conflict) and is skipped on future assignments.

params.threat: DHCP could lease these to clients → IP conflict

hack.memory: ALWAYS exclude the gateway IP. Failing to exclude .1 = guaranteed DHCP conflict the moment a client grabs it. Block out a whole static range (e.g., .1-.20) so future statics don't collide with DHCP.
hack.practice: Sketch a 10.1.1.0/24 subnet on paper. Mark .1 = gateway, .5 = print server, .10 = access point. Write the exact exclusion command(s) that protect them: 'ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10'. Single range covers them all. This becomes habit.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam may show output where a client has the same IP as the gateway — root cause is missing exclusion. 'ip dhcp conflict' can catch it after the fact, but prevention is the real answer.

4.6.e.info:
Exclude syntax has two forms: a single address or a contiguous range. Both work in the same global config command.

Single address: ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 — protects exactly one IP. Useful when you have one static device and don't want to block a whole range.

Range: ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10 — protects every IP from start to end, inclusive. The start must be numerically ≤ the end; IOS rejects reversed ranges.

For multiple non-contiguous ranges, stack separate statements:

ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.100 10.1.1.110
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.240

IOS handles overlapping ranges gracefully — the union of all exclusions is what's reserved. You can also use the range form for IPv6 prefix exclusions with ipv6 dhcp excluded-address, but IPv6 has its own SLAAC-heavy addressing model and exclusion is less common.

Check exclusions with show running-config | include excluded (lists every statement) or show ip dhcp pool (shows the pool's effective size after exclusions). If numbers look wrong (too many excluded, not enough leasable), compare the output against what you meant to configure.

Removing exclusions: no ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10. You must specify the exact same range you originally entered — you can't remove a subset of a range.

hack.memory: Exclude syntax: 'ip dhcp excluded-address <start> [end]'. One address or a range. Multiple statements stack. Can't remove half a range — remove the full range you originally typed.
hack.practice: Configure 3 exclusions on a single pool: one single IP, one range, one non-contiguous second range. Verify with 'show running-config | include excluded' and 'show ip dhcp pool'. Remove one and watch the pool size grow. Every combination builds muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam: know the syntax forms (single + range), know it's global not pool config, and know that 'no' requires the exact same argument.

4.6.f.info:
show ip dhcp binding is the DHCP server's lease table — the authoritative list of who currently has which IP, matched by client hardware address (MAC). Output looks like:

IP address      Client-ID/             Lease expiration        Type
                Hardware address/
                User name
10.1.1.11       0100.1234.5678.90      Mar 15 2026 10:22       Automatic
10.1.1.12       0100.aabb.ccdd.eeff    Mar 15 2026 10:30       Automatic

The Client-ID column contains the client's hardware address prefixed by a hardware type byte (01 = Ethernet). You match the MAC by stripping the leading '01'. Example: 0100.1234.5678.90 → MAC 0012.3456.7890. The type column shows Automatic for DHCP-assigned leases, Manual for static reservations, and Relay for leases served through a DHCP relay.

Exam scenarios: you get this output and have to answer 'which IP did MAC X get?' or 'when does MAC Y's lease expire?'. It's pure table lookup — practice reading the columns until it's automatic. Remember to account for the 01 prefix in the Client-ID.

Related commands: show ip dhcp binding [ip] filters to a single binding. clear ip dhcp binding [ip] or clear ip dhcp binding * forcibly releases a lease (client will renew and possibly get a new IP). Useful for testing and for forcing renewal after an exclusion change.

Lease counts too high? Lease time too long for your environment? Check current bindings before lowering the lease — clients with existing long leases won't renew until their half-life, so changes take effect gradually.

hack.memory: 'show ip dhcp binding' = the lease table. Client-ID has a hardware-type prefix '01' before the MAC — strip it to match the client's MAC. Type: Automatic = DHCP, Manual = reservation, Relay = came through a relay agent.
hack.practice: Run 'show ip dhcp binding' in Packet Tracer. Pick any row and check the PC's MAC with 'ipconfig /all' — drop the '01.' and you'll see the MACs match. Do this 5x until the Client-ID format is automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Favorite exam prop: a binding table screenshot and 'which IP has MAC 0011.2233.4455?' — answer requires stripping the 01 prefix from Client-ID.

4.6.f.info:
show ip dhcp pool shows pool-level statistics — how big the pool is, how many addresses are currently leased, and how many were excluded. Output looks like:

Pool LAN :
 Utilization mark (high/low)    : 100 / 0
 Subnet size (first/next)       : 0 / 0
 Total addresses                : 254
 Leased addresses               : 12
 Excluded addresses             : 10
 Pending event                  : none
 1 subnet is currently in the pool :
 Current index    IP address range               Leased/Excluded/Total
 10.1.1.11        10.1.1.1    - 10.1.1.254        12     / 10    / 254

Key numbers: Total = all addresses in the network minus the broadcast (a /24 gives 254 usable). Leased = active bindings. Excluded = addresses protected by ip dhcp excluded-address. The effective pool size for DHCP is Total - Excluded.

Utilization mark triggers alerts (syslog messages) when lease usage crosses the high-mark threshold. Defaults are 100/0 (never alert). Shops with capacity-management practices set marks like 85/70 to get warned before the pool runs out.

Troubleshooting use: if clients stop getting IPs and 'Leased = Total', your pool is exhausted. Either expand the subnet, reduce lease time, or release stale bindings with clear ip dhcp binding *.

Multiple pools on one router? Run show ip dhcp pool [name] to filter, or just show ip dhcp pool to list every pool the router owns.

hack.memory: 'show ip dhcp pool' = pool capacity report. Watch Total vs Leased vs Excluded. Pool exhausted = clients can't get IPs. Fix = bigger subnet, shorter leases, or clear stale bindings.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: shrink the pool by excluding a big range, then keep connecting clients until DHCP fails. Observe 'show ip dhcp pool' as Leased approaches Total minus Excluded. Now run 'clear ip dhcp binding *' and watch Leased reset to 0.
hack.meta: Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Exam less likely to show this exact output than 'show ip dhcp binding', but know what each column means for troubleshooting scenarios.

4.6.f.info:
show ip dhcp conflict lists IPs that the DHCP server has detected as conflicted — meaning the server pinged the IP before handing it out and got a response, or a client reported a DHCPDECLINE because it couldn't use the address it was offered.

Cisco DHCP servers ping-probe addresses before leasing them (ip dhcp ping packets 2, 2 packets by default with a 500ms timeout). If the probe gets a response, the server logs the IP as conflicted and refuses to hand it out. Normal pool operation skips over conflicted IPs automatically.

Sample output:

IP address       Detection method    Detection time
10.1.1.15        Ping                Mar 15 2026 10:00
10.1.1.18        Gratuitous ARP      Mar 15 2026 10:05

Detection methods: Ping — server's probe got a response before lease. Gratuitous ARP — a client sent a gratuitous ARP showing it already owns that IP. Relay-Agent (rare) — the relay reported a conflict.

Clearing conflicts: clear ip dhcp conflict * or clear ip dhcp conflict [ip] removes entries from the conflict list. The server can then re-attempt handing that address out on the next lease request. Only clear conflicts after you've fixed the underlying duplicate-IP situation — otherwise the conflict just reappears.

Use case: conflicts usually indicate someone assigned a static IP inside the DHCP range without an exclusion. Audit: who owns the conflicted IP statically? Add it to ip dhcp excluded-address, clear the conflict, and move on.

hack.memory: 'show ip dhcp conflict' = IPs the server found already in use (via ping or gratuitous ARP). Server auto-skips them. Real fix: exclude the IP and hunt down the rogue static device. Clear conflicts only after fixing the root cause.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: statically assign a PC to 10.1.1.15 (inside the DHCP pool, not excluded). Request a new DHCP lease from another PC. Run 'show ip dhcp conflict' — 10.1.1.15 shows up. Add exclusion, clear conflict, remove static, and try again.
hack.meta: Less common on the exam but worth knowing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 8. Detection methods: Ping vs Gratuitous ARP — both are tested occasionally.

4.7.a.info:
QoS isn't a single feature — it's a pipeline. Packets pass through stages in a specific order, and each stage builds on the previous one. The canonical order is: Classification → Marking → Queuing → Policing/Shaping → Congestion avoidance. Memorize this order cold — the exam tests it directly.

Classification identifies traffic types (voice, video, transactional, bulk). Marking tags packets with a DSCP or CoS value so downstream devices know the priority without re-classifying. Queuing puts traffic into different output queues based on marking (voice in LLQ, data in CBWFQ, etc.). Policing/Shaping enforces rate limits — policing drops excess, shaping buffers it. Congestion avoidance (WRED) prevents queue tail-drop by proactively dropping low-priority packets before the queue fills.

Classification and marking should happen as close to the traffic source as possible — at the access switch or IP phone. This is the trust boundary. Beyond that boundary, every device just reads the mark and acts. Marking once at the edge is vastly more efficient than classifying at every hop.

A packet might skip stages. Best-effort traffic isn't classified at all — it gets default treatment throughout. Low-traffic networks never hit congestion so they never engage WRED. But high-value traffic (voice, video) goes through all five stages every hop.

Why order matters: you can't mark what you haven't classified. You can't queue what you haven't marked. You can't police what you haven't identified. Reverse any step and the pipeline breaks. CCNA test question format: 'Match each QoS action to its pipeline step' or 'Which step comes immediately after classification?' (marking).

hack.memory: QoS order: CMQPC — Classify, Mark, Queue, Police/Shape, Congestion-avoid. Or mnemonic: 'Cops Make Quiet People Cry'. Classify first because you can't prioritize what you haven't identified.
hack.practice: Draw the 5-stage pipeline as a flowchart. Put example traffic through it: voice call from a SIP phone → classified as voice (port 5060 / RTP) → marked EF/46 → queued in LLQ → policed at 1 Mbps per call → never hits WRED (it's in the priority queue). This mental walkthrough is the easiest way to internalize the order.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Guaranteed exam question about pipeline order. You will not configure QoS on CCNA — pure concepts.

4.7.a.info:
Classification is the step where the router or switch decides 'what kind of traffic is this?'. Every subsequent QoS action (marking, queuing, policing) depends on classification. Without it, all traffic gets the same best-effort treatment.

Classification methods, from simplest to most intelligent: (1) Incoming interface — all traffic from Gi0/0 is classified as X. Crude but useful at trust boundaries. (2) Port number — UDP 5060 = SIP signaling, UDP 16384-32767 = RTP voice. Fast but fooled by non-standard ports. (3) ACLs — match by source/destination IP and port. More granular. (4) DSCP/CoS trust — honor the existing marking from a trusted upstream device (IP phone, another router inside the trust boundary). (5) NBAR (Network-Based Application Recognition) — deep packet inspection that identifies applications by signature even when they use dynamic ports (Skype, BitTorrent, WebEx, etc.).

NBAR is the heaviest tool. It's Cisco's DPI engine, updated via Protocol Pack downloads. It can distinguish HTTP video streaming from HTTP file downloads, identify specific SaaS apps, and catch evasive protocols. Costs CPU and memory — more expensive than ACL-based classification. NBAR2 adds even deeper application visibility.

Classification happens once at the trust boundary; after that, devices rely on the marking rather than re-classifying. This is both a performance optimization (re-classifying is expensive) and a consistency guarantee (one classification policy, enforced everywhere).

CCNA scope: know the classification methods and that NBAR is DPI. You won't configure class-maps. Expect questions like 'which classification method uses deep packet inspection?' (NBAR) or 'which is the most granular way to classify traffic?' (NBAR; next-best is ACLs).

hack.memory: Classification = 'what kind of traffic is this?'. Methods range from dumb (incoming interface) to smart (NBAR deep packet inspection). Classify once at the trust boundary; everywhere else just trusts the mark.
hack.practice: Flashcard: list the 5 classification methods and one pro/con each. NBAR = most accurate but most CPU. Port = fast but fooled by non-standard ports. ACL = precise but manual maintenance. Trust = cheap if you trust the upstream.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam favorite: 'which classification method uses DPI?' = NBAR. Also: 'why classify at the trust boundary?' = so downstream devices can rely on the mark.

4.7.a.info:
Marking writes a priority value into the frame/packet header so every downstream device knows how to treat it without re-classifying. Classification identifies; marking records the decision on the packet itself.

Two primary marking fields: CoS (Class of Service) — 3 bits in the 802.1Q VLAN tag, values 0-7, lives at Layer 2, lost when the frame is routed or untagged. DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) — 6 bits in the IP header's ToS byte, values 0-63, lives at Layer 3, survives across router hops and into the WAN.

Marking happens at the trust boundary (typically the access switch). An IP phone sends its voice RTP with CoS=5 or DSCP=EF already set; the switch trusts the phone and preserves the mark. A PC behind the phone sends data with its own DSCP; the switch does NOT trust the PC and re-marks that traffic to best-effort or re-classifies and re-marks based on its own policy.

Inside a routed network, DSCP is king because it survives every hop. CoS is useful only within a single VLAN or across trunk links; it vanishes the moment the frame is L3-forwarded. For end-to-end QoS across a WAN, you must use DSCP or re-mark at each boundary.

One packet carries both markings simultaneously: DSCP in the IP header AND CoS in the 802.1Q tag (on trunk links). Switches and routers can copy DSCP into CoS (or vice versa) at boundary points so both marks stay in sync. This mapping is called CoS-to-DSCP translation.

segments.range: L3 IP ToS byte (3 high bits)

hack.memory: Marking = writing the priority on the packet itself. CoS = L2 sticker (lost at routing). DSCP = L3 stamp (survives routing). Mark once at the trust boundary; every hop reads and acts.
hack.practice: Sketch an IP phone + PC + access switch + router + WAN scenario. On the phone-switch link, both CoS and DSCP exist. On the switch-router-WAN path, only DSCP survives. This diagram shows why DSCP is the real end-to-end marker.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'which marking survives across routers?' = DSCP. 'How many bits is CoS?' = 3. 'How many is DSCP?' = 6.

4.7.a.info:
Per-Hop Behavior (PHB) is the DiffServ architecture's core concept: each router along the path independently applies QoS treatment based on the packet's DSCP marking. There's no end-to-end signaling protocol negotiating priorities — each hop just reads the mark and does its local thing.

This is the opposite of IntServ (RSVP), which tries to reserve bandwidth end-to-end across every device. IntServ doesn't scale. DiffServ with PHB is how the real internet and enterprise networks do QoS.

Standard PHBs defined by RFCs: EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46) — low latency, low loss, low jitter; used for voice. AF (Assured Forwarding, DSCP 10-43) — 4 classes × 3 drop precedences, used for video and data with different priority/drop combinations. CS (Class Selector, DSCP 0-56 in 8 increments) — backward-compatible with IP Precedence. Default / Best Effort (DSCP 0) — no QoS.

For PHBs to work end-to-end, every router must implement the same DSCP-to-treatment mapping. If R1 treats DSCP 46 as priority but R2 treats DSCP 46 as best-effort, voice quality collapses at R2. This requires a consistent QoS policy across the domain — a typical source of service-provider/enterprise QoS debates.

CCNA focus: know that PHB means 'each router independently applies QoS based on DSCP'. Know the standard PHBs (EF, AF, CS, Default). Know that end-to-end consistency depends on every device honoring the same marking.

hack.memory: PHB = each router decides QoS treatment based on DSCP, independently. No end-to-end negotiation. If any hop ignores DSCP or uses a different mapping, end-to-end QoS breaks. Standard PHBs: EF, AF, CS, Default.
hack.practice: Think through a voice call traversing 5 routers. Every router reads DSCP=46 and puts the packet in its priority queue. If router 3 is misconfigured and treats DSCP=46 as best-effort, the call quality degrades there. This is why consistent PHB policy across the network matters.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what is a Per-Hop Behavior?' = each router independently applies QoS based on marking. Know EF/AF/CS/Default as the main PHBs.

4.7.a.info:
Unlike SSH or DHCP, QoS on the CCNA 200-301 exam is purely conceptual. You will not be asked to configure a class-map, a policy-map, or a service-policy. No MQC CLI. No shaping or policing configuration. No trust-boundary CLI. The exam tests understanding of WHAT each QoS piece does, not HOW to configure it.

Your study list: (1) The processing order (Classify → Mark → Queue → Police/Shape → Avoid congestion). (2) Classification methods (ACL, port, NBAR, trust). (3) Marking fields (CoS, DSCP, IP Precedence). (4) Key DSCP values (EF=46 voice, AF classes, CS, Default=0). (5) Queuing mechanisms (LLQ, CBWFQ, WFQ, FIFO). (6) Congestion avoidance (WRED, tail drop, TCP global sync). (7) Policing vs Shaping (drops vs buffers). (8) Trust boundary placement.

Expected exam question types: 'Which queuing mechanism provides strict priority?' (LLQ). 'What does WRED prevent?' (Tail drop, TCP global synchronization). 'Which marking survives routing?' (DSCP). 'What does shaping do with excess traffic?' (Buffer it). 'Where should the trust boundary be?' (As close to the source as possible, typically the access switch).

Because it's concept-only, your study strategy is flashcards and diagrams, not lab hours. A single 2-hour focused session covering every QoS concept, with comparison tables (policing vs shaping, CoS vs DSCP, LLQ vs CBWFQ), typically nails the 3-5 QoS exam questions.

CCNP Enterprise ENCOR goes deeper and includes hands-on configuration. For CCNA, stop at concepts.

hack.memory: CCNA QoS = concepts only. No CLI tested. Flashcard the pipeline, marking fields, DSCP values, queuing mechanisms, WRED, policing-vs-shaping, and trust boundary. Skip the config chapters.
hack.practice: Build 5 comparison flashcards: (1) CoS vs DSCP, (2) LLQ vs CBWFQ, (3) Policing vs Shaping, (4) Tail drop vs WRED, (5) Trust boundary location rule. These 5 cards cover ~90% of CCNA QoS questions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46 confirms concept-only scope for CCNA. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Spend time on concepts, not labs.

4.7.b.info:
CoS (Class of Service) is a Layer 2 QoS marking that lives inside the 802.1Q VLAN tag. Specifically, it occupies the 3-bit PCP (Priority Code Point) field, giving 8 possible values (0-7). The IEEE 802.1p standard defines the meanings (0=best effort, 5=voice, 6=internetwork control, 7=network control).

CoS only exists where an 802.1Q tag exists — that means trunk links between switches and certain access ports (like ports connected to IP phones that receive the voice VLAN via 802.1Q). On untagged access ports, there's no CoS field to carry. When a switch removes the 802.1Q tag (e.g., forwarding to an access port), the CoS marking is lost.

When a frame crosses a Layer 3 boundary (a router), the router strips the original L2 frame entirely and rebuilds a new one for the outgoing interface. The CoS value from the old frame is gone. If downstream routers need priority info, they must rely on Layer 3 markings (DSCP) or the switch must pre-copy CoS into DSCP before routing.

Standard CoS-to-traffic-type mappings (IEEE 802.1p recommendations): 0 = best effort, 1 = background, 2 = excellent effort, 3 = critical apps, 4 = video, 5 = voice, 6 = internetwork control, 7 = network control. These are recommendations, not hard rules — your network might use different mappings, but using the standards avoids interop pain.

Use case: between switches in the same switched domain, CoS is fast and cheap to process. Voice VLANs on IP phones typically trust the phone's CoS=5 marking for voice traffic.

hack.memory: CoS = 3 bits in 802.1Q tag = 8 values (0-7). Layer 2 only. Dies when the frame is routed or untagged. Standard voice = CoS 5.
hack.practice: Wireshark an 802.1Q tagged frame. Expand the 802.1Q header — the PCP field is the CoS value. Change a switch port from access to trunk and watch the CoS field appear on captured traffic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'how many bits is CoS?' = 3. 'Where does CoS live?' = 802.1Q tag. 'Does CoS survive routing?' = no.

4.7.b.info:
DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) is the modern Layer 3 QoS marking. It lives in the 6 most-significant bits of the IP header's ToS byte (the remaining 2 bits are used for ECN — Explicit Congestion Notification). 6 bits = 64 possible values (0-63).

Key advantage over CoS: DSCP is in the IP header, which doesn't get rewritten at Layer 3 hops. A packet marked DSCP=46 at an access switch will still be DSCP=46 when it reaches a server on the other side of a WAN, as long as no device along the way strips or rewrites the marking. This makes DSCP the standard for end-to-end QoS.

DSCP values organize into well-known categories: EF (46) for voice, AF classes (10-43) for video/data, CS values (0, 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56) for backward compat with IP Precedence, and Default (0) for best effort. These are defined by RFCs and form the DiffServ PHB vocabulary.

DSCP works in both IPv4 and IPv6. In IPv4 it's the top 6 bits of the ToS byte. In IPv6 it's the top 6 bits of the Traffic Class field. Same values, same meanings.

Operationally: mark DSCP at the trust boundary (the access switch or IP phone), trust DSCP at every downstream router, and let the WAN QoS policies act on it. If any device along the way strips DSCP (some WAN providers do this on low-cost services), you lose end-to-end QoS. Ask your provider about DSCP preservation before depending on it across their network.

hack.memory: DSCP = 6 bits in IP header = 64 values (0-63). Layer 3. Survives routing end-to-end. EF=46 for voice is the one value you must memorize cold.
hack.practice: Wireshark a packet from a VoIP call. Expand the IP header. Find the DSCP field — it should be 46 (EF) for RTP voice. This single capture cements DSCP location and meaning.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'how many bits is DSCP?' = 6. 'Does DSCP survive routing?' = yes. 'What's EF?' = DSCP 46, voice.

4.7.b.info:
CoS vs DSCP is the classic QoS comparison question. Here's the full breakdown:

Layer: CoS = L2. DSCP = L3. Field: CoS lives in the 802.1Q VLAN tag (PCP bits). DSCP lives in the IP header ToS byte. Bits: CoS = 3 bits. DSCP = 6 bits. Values: CoS = 0-7 (8 values). DSCP = 0-63 (64 values). Persistence: CoS is lost at L3 boundaries (when a router re-encapsulates) or when the 802.1Q tag is stripped (untagged access port). DSCP survives every router hop.

When to use each: CoS is fine within a single switched/VLAN environment. DSCP is required for end-to-end QoS across a routed network or WAN. Most modern designs mark DSCP at the source and use CoS only as a transient L2 signaling between switches.

CoS-to-DSCP mapping: Switches often map CoS values from 802.1Q frames to corresponding DSCP values in the IP header so both L2 and L3 devices see the same priority. Default Cisco mapping: CoS 5 → DSCP 46 (EF, voice), CoS 3 → DSCP 24 (CS3, signaling), CoS 0 → DSCP 0 (default). This mapping can be customized.

Common exam question: 'Which marking survives when a frame is routed?' — answer is always DSCP. 'Which marking is used only within Layer 2?' — CoS.

hack.memory: CoS = L2, 3 bits, 0-7, 802.1Q tag, lost at routing. DSCP = L3, 6 bits, 0-63, IP header, survives routing. If the question has 'end-to-end' or 'across routers', the answer is always DSCP.
hack.practice: Build one comparison flashcard with the 5 rows above. Recite both sides in under 10 seconds. This is the single most important QoS flashcard.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam question. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Memorize the table — it answers every CoS vs DSCP question variant.

4.7.b.info:
IP Precedence (IPP) is the legacy Layer 3 QoS marking, originally defined in RFC 791. It uses the top 3 bits of the ToS byte in the IPv4 header — giving 8 values (0-7). Exactly the same real estate that DSCP's top 3 bits now occupy.

Historical context: IP Precedence was the original QoS marking. It worked, but 8 values weren't granular enough for modern applications. The DiffServ working group expanded the field to 6 bits (64 values) and redefined it as DSCP. To maintain backward compatibility, DSCP defined the Class Selector (CS) values — DSCP values where the bottom 3 bits are 0, so the top 3 bits match an equivalent IPP value.

IPP-to-CS mapping:

IP Prec 0 (routine)           → CS0 → DSCP 0
IP Prec 1 (priority)          → CS1 → DSCP 8
IP Prec 2 (immediate)         → CS2 → DSCP 16
IP Prec 3 (flash)             → CS3 → DSCP 24
IP Prec 4 (flash override)    → CS4 → DSCP 32
IP Prec 5 (critical)          → CS5 → DSCP 40
IP Prec 6 (internetwork ctrl) → CS6 → DSCP 48
IP Prec 7 (network control)   → CS7 → DSCP 56

You'll occasionally see IPP in older router/switch configurations, ISP contracts, or legacy applications. Modern networks are almost entirely DSCP. For CCNA, just know that IPP is the legacy 3-bit L3 marking and that CS values are the DSCP values that map 1-to-1 with IPP.

Practical note: if you have old gear that only understands IPP and new gear that speaks DSCP, configure CS values on the DSCP side — both worlds see the same priority bits.

layers: CS values use only bits 0-2, match IPP

hack.memory: IP Precedence = legacy L3 marking. 3 bits, 0-7. Same position as DSCP's top 3 bits. CS0-CS7 are the DSCP values that mirror IPP. Mostly academic knowledge — modern networks use DSCP directly.
hack.practice: Quick flashcard: map IPP 5 → CS5 → DSCP 40. Know this mapping exists so you can parse old docs or answer backward-compat exam questions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what replaces IP Precedence?' = DSCP. 'Why does DSCP have CS values?' = for backward compatibility with IPP.

4.7.c.info:
EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46) is the single most important DSCP value to memorize. It's reserved for real-time traffic that absolutely cannot tolerate delay, jitter, or loss — voice (VoIP RTP streams) and sometimes critical video conferencing.

Defined in RFC 3246, EF represents the highest-priority forwarding behavior a network offers. Devices implementing EF guarantee low latency, low jitter, and low loss for packets marked DSCP 46. In practice, EF-marked traffic goes into a strict priority queue (LLQ) that's always serviced first, ahead of everything else.

Voice requirements that EF serves: one-way delay ≤ 150 ms, jitter ≤ 30 ms, packet loss ≤ 1%. These come from the G.114 ITU recommendation for voice quality. Missing any of them degrades call quality quickly.

Why DSCP 46? The binary is 101110. Reserved in the DiffServ RFC specifically for EF. Most Cisco IOS defaults (class-map match any EF, priority queue commands) key off DSCP 46 automatically.

Design implication: EF traffic must be policed. If you dump unlimited EF traffic into the priority queue, it can starve every other queue — data transfers stop, video lags, everything grinds. Typical design: allow EF up to a percentage of link bandwidth (often 33%) and police anything beyond that. A single voice call is ~100 Kbps with G.711 codec; a link can support many calls before hitting the EF cap.

hack.memory: EF = 46 = Voice. 'Express Freeway' — always in the strict-priority lane. Memorize DSCP 46 = EF = voice. Voice needs ≤150ms delay, ≤30ms jitter, ≤1% loss.
hack.practice: Capture a live voice call in Wireshark. Every RTP packet should be DSCP 46 (EF). Confirm this one time and it'll stick forever.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Guaranteed exam question: 'what DSCP value is used for voice?' = 46. 'What traffic uses EF?' = voice / real-time.

4.7.c.info:
AF (Assured Forwarding) is the DiffServ PHB for traffic that needs a bandwidth guarantee but isn't as time-sensitive as voice. Defined in RFC 2597. Organized as 4 classes × 3 drop precedences = 12 distinct DSCP values (AF11, AF12, AF13, AF21, AF22, AF23, AF31, AF32, AF33, AF41, AF42, AF43).

Naming convention: AFXY where X = class (1-4) and Y = drop probability (1=low drop, 2=medium, 3=high). Class 4 is the highest priority; Class 1 is the lowest. Within a class, the drop probability controls who dies first during congestion — AF11 (low drop) survives; AF13 (high drop) is tossed first.

AF decimal values (memorize only if you're aiming for a perfect score):

AF11 = 10    AF21 = 18    AF31 = 26    AF41 = 34
AF12 = 12    AF22 = 20    AF32 = 28    AF42 = 36
AF13 = 14    AF23 = 22    AF33 = 30    AF43 = 38

Pattern: first digit indicates class (multiply by 8 for the base), second digit (1/2/3) adds 2/4/6 for drop probability. For CCNA you mainly need to know the structure, not every decimal.

Typical usage: AF41 = high-priority video conferencing. AF31 = call signaling (SIP). AF21 = transactional data (Oracle, SAP). AF11 = bulk data (backups, file transfers). Each class has its own CBWFQ queue with a guaranteed bandwidth percentage.

hack.memory: AF = 4 classes × 3 drop precedences = 12 values. First digit = class (priority: 4>3>2>1). Second digit = drop prob (1=survive, 3=die first). AF41 = high-pri video. AF11 = bulk data.
hack.practice: Sketch a 4x3 grid. Rows = classes 1-4 (priority). Columns = drop probs 1-3. Fill in the 12 AF names. Redo from memory a few times until the structure sticks. Don't memorize decimals unless you're chasing extra points.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam tests the structure (4 classes, 3 drops), not every decimal value. Know AF41 = highest video priority, AF43 = most likely to be dropped in class 4.

4.7.c.info:
CS (Class Selector) DSCP values provide backward compatibility with the legacy 3-bit IP Precedence marking. Defined in RFC 2474. CS values use only the top 3 bits of the DSCP field (the bottom 3 are always 0), which exactly matches where IP Precedence used to live.

The 8 CS values:

CS0 = DSCP 0   (IPP 0 = routine / best effort)
CS1 = DSCP 8   (IPP 1 = priority)
CS2 = DSCP 16  (IPP 2 = immediate)
CS3 = DSCP 24  (IPP 3 = flash)
CS4 = DSCP 32  (IPP 4 = flash override)
CS5 = DSCP 40  (IPP 5 = critical / voice signaling)
CS6 = DSCP 48  (IPP 6 = internetwork control — routing protocols)
CS7 = DSCP 56  (IPP 7 = network control — STP, BPDU)

The arithmetic: CSN = N × 8. CS3 = 3×8 = 24. CS6 = 6×8 = 48. Easy memorization once you see the pattern.

Practical use today: CS6 is commonly marked on routing protocol traffic (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP hellos) to ensure those packets don't get queued behind bulk data. CS7 for spanning-tree BPDUs. CS5 sometimes for call signaling. CS0 is just best-effort (same as DSCP 0 and Default).

CCNA scope: know that CS values map 1:1 with IP Precedence and use the pattern CSN = N × 8. You won't be asked to memorize every decimal value. Know that CS6 is typical for routing protocol traffic.

hack.memory: CS = Class Selector. 8 values, 0-7 as IPP compat. Formula: CSN = N × 8. CS6 = 48 (routing protocols). CS7 = 56 (network control like STP).
hack.practice: Fill in the CS table from memory using CSN = N × 8. Do it 3 times. This covers CCNA's CS-value questions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam might ask 'what is CS6 used for?' = internetwork control / routing protocols. Know the CSN × 8 formula.

4.7.c.info:
DSCP 0 is the default/best-effort marking. Every IP packet starts life as DSCP 0 unless some device along the way marks it. Packets that haven't been classified, packets from untrusted devices, and packets on networks with no QoS policy all carry DSCP 0.

Best effort means exactly that: no priority, no guarantees, first-in-first-out with everyone else in the default queue. Best-effort traffic gets whatever bandwidth is left after priority queues and bandwidth-reserved queues have been served.

DSCP 0 is also CS0 (Class Selector 0) and sometimes called BE (Best Effort). All three terms refer to the same DSCP value. RFC naming overlap.

Why it matters: when a trust boundary re-marks untrusted traffic, it usually resets DSCP to 0. This demotes unauthorized high-priority claims to best-effort. A PC trying to mark its own traffic as EF gets its DSCP reset to 0 at the access switch so it can't abuse the priority queue.

In routing terms: DSCP 0 is the universal baseline. Every network device knows it. No configuration is required to forward DSCP 0 traffic. It's the unconfigured, unspecial default — which is exactly why identifying the priority traffic (voice, video, critical apps) and marking it above 0 is what QoS is really about.

hack.memory: DSCP 0 = Default = Best Effort = CS0. All traffic starts here. Trust boundary re-marks untrusted endpoint traffic back to 0 so they can't game QoS.
hack.practice: Capture any random web or DNS packet. DSCP will almost always be 0 unless the app specifically marks itself. This confirms 'most traffic is best-effort by default'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what is DSCP 0?' = default / best effort. Sometimes written as CS0 or BE — same thing.

4.7.d.info:
LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) is a strict priority queue added on top of CBWFQ. Traffic in the LLQ is ALWAYS sent before any other queue — the scheduler never services a non-LLQ queue while the LLQ has packets waiting. This gives the lowest possible latency and jitter, which is exactly what voice needs.

Why voice needs LLQ: voice packets arrive every 20ms from a phone. If a bulk data transfer is ahead of them in the queue, the voice packet waits. Waits = latency. Variable waits = jitter. Either breaks the call. LLQ bypasses the wait by preempting the queue — voice goes out immediately.

Built-in policer: LLQ includes a bandwidth cap (often expressed as a percentage of link bandwidth, e.g., priority 33 = 33% of the link). If priority traffic exceeds the cap, LLQ drops the excess. This prevents LLQ from starving other queues. Without the policer, unlimited priority traffic could consume 100% of bandwidth and leave nothing for data.

Typical deployment: one LLQ for voice (EF, DSCP 46), sometimes a second LLQ for critical video (AF41). Everything else goes into CBWFQ queues with bandwidth guarantees. Default traffic gets whatever's left.

Configuration concept (not tested on CCNA): class voice; priority percent 33 inside a policy-map. The 'priority' keyword makes it an LLQ vs 'bandwidth' which makes it a CBWFQ queue.

Design rule of thumb: keep LLQ small — 33% of bandwidth is a common ceiling. More than that often means voice is bleeding into data's budget and you need more bandwidth on the link.

hack.memory: LLQ = strict priority + built-in policer. Voice goes FIRST, every time. Policer caps it so it doesn't starve everything else. Typical cap: 33% of link BW.
hack.practice: Draw the output scheduler: one LLQ box at the top (voice), multiple CBWFQ boxes below (video, data, default). Arrow labeled 'serviced first' from scheduler to LLQ. Put a policer symbol on the LLQ input. This diagram is worth 2 exam questions by itself.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'which queuing provides strict priority?' = LLQ. 'What prevents LLQ from starving other queues?' = built-in policer.

4.7.d.info:
CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing) assigns guaranteed minimum bandwidth to different traffic classes. Each class configured with CBWFQ gets a reserved percentage (or Kbps) of the interface bandwidth during congestion. When no congestion exists, any class can burst above its reservation using free bandwidth.

How it works during congestion: the scheduler services each CBWFQ queue based on its weight (configured bandwidth share). A 'video' class with bandwidth percent 30 is guaranteed 30% of the link even if data traffic is trying to use it all. Inside each class, packets are sent in FIFO order by default (or with fair queuing if the interface type supports it).

CBWFQ vs fair-share: without CBWFQ, the default queueing on a congested interface (FIFO or WFQ) gives no guarantees — a bulk data transfer can monopolize the link. CBWFQ carves out per-class reservations so critical apps always have a share, even under load.

CBWFQ combines with LLQ in nearly every real policy: LLQ for voice (strict priority), CBWFQ for video / transactional / bulk (bandwidth reservations), default class for everything else.

'Weighted' in CBWFQ refers to the bandwidth weight assigned to each class. Higher weight = more bandwidth share during congestion. The 'class-based' part means matching is done by class-maps (ACLs, DSCP values, NBAR output, etc.) rather than flat FIFO or per-flow WFQ.

CCNA won't ask you to configure CBWFQ. Know: CBWFQ guarantees minimum bandwidth per class. That one sentence answers most CBWFQ exam questions.

hack.memory: CBWFQ = Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing = guaranteed minimum bandwidth per class. During congestion each class gets its reserved share. Sits next to LLQ in typical policies.
hack.practice: Given an interface with 100 Mbps: LLQ voice = 20%, CBWFQ video = 30%, CBWFQ data = 20%, default = remaining. Calculate: 20M voice, 30M video, 20M data, 30M default. This is what CBWFQ guarantees under congestion.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what queuing gives bandwidth guarantees?' = CBWFQ. 'Difference between LLQ and CBWFQ?' = LLQ is strict priority, CBWFQ is bandwidth reservation.

4.7.d.info:
FIFO (First-In, First-Out) is the simplest possible queuing discipline: one queue, packets serviced strictly in arrival order. No prioritization, no bandwidth reservation. The first packet in the door is the first packet out.

FIFO is the default queueing on high-speed interfaces (Ethernet ≥ 2.048 Mbps). On faster links, congestion is rare, so the overhead of more complex queuing isn't worth it. FIFO's simplicity makes it extremely fast and predictable under normal load.

The downside: when congestion DOES happen on a FIFO interface, all traffic is treated equally. A voice call's RTP packets wait behind a large file transfer. Latency spikes, jitter explodes, voice quality tanks. FIFO has no concept of priority.

Large packets create 'head-of-line blocking' in FIFO: a 1500-byte packet ahead of a 200-byte voice packet means the voice waits until the 1500-byte packet finishes transmitting. On a 1 Mbps link, 1500 bytes = 12 ms of delay. On a 64 Kbps link, 1500 bytes = 187 ms of delay — already violating voice's 150 ms budget.

When to use FIFO: high-speed, uncongested interfaces where QoS overhead isn't justified. When to replace FIFO: any link carrying real-time traffic (voice, video) or any link that gets congested. The replacement is usually LLQ + CBWFQ.

hack.memory: FIFO = First-In-First-Out = one queue, arrival order, no priority. Default on fast interfaces. Awful when congested — large packets block small ones. Replace with LLQ+CBWFQ if voice/video or congestion is present.
hack.practice: Simulate on paper: a 1 Mbps link, voice packet arrives right behind a 1500-byte data packet. Voice waits 12 ms. Add more data packets and watch voice latency grow. This is why FIFO + voice = bad.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what's the default queuing on Ethernet?' = FIFO. 'Why is FIFO bad for voice?' = no prioritization, head-of-line blocking.

4.7.d.info:
Tail drop is what happens when a queue fills up: every new packet arriving at the tail of the queue is dropped, regardless of priority or class. It's the simplest congestion response — also the dumbest.

Tail drop treats all packets equally at the point of failure. Voice packet, video packet, backup packet — if the queue is full, they all get dropped. No DSCP inspection, no class awareness. This is unacceptable for QoS networks where priority traffic must survive congestion.

Worse: tail drop causes TCP global synchronization. When a queue fills, it drops packets from every TCP flow simultaneously. Every sender detects loss at the same time, backs off its window simultaneously, and resumes sending simultaneously. The result is oscillating congestion — bandwidth goes from saturated to nearly idle and back in waves, wasting capacity.

Solution: WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection). WRED starts dropping packets BEFORE the queue is full, and drops selectively by priority (lower priority = dropped first). This prevents tail drop and staggers TCP backoffs so flows don't all sync up.

Tail drop is still a fallback even with WRED — if packets arrive faster than WRED can drop them, the queue still fills and tail drop kicks in. In practice, well-tuned WRED keeps queues below the tail-drop threshold under normal congestion.

states: TCP flows detect loss simultaneously → global sync

hack.memory: Tail drop = queue full, drop everything new regardless of priority. Indiscriminate. Causes TCP global synchronization. WRED is the fix — drop proactively, selectively.
hack.practice: Draw a queue filling up. At 100% utilization, draw arrows showing new packets (voice, video, data) all getting rejected equally. That's tail drop. Now draw WRED: at 70% utilization, start dropping low-priority packets proactively so the queue never fills.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what is tail drop?' = queue full, new packets dropped. 'What problem does it cause?' = TCP global synchronization. 'What fixes it?' = WRED.

4.7.e.info:
WRED (Weighted Random Early Detection) is a congestion-avoidance mechanism that prevents tail drop by proactively dropping packets BEFORE the queue fills. Instead of waiting until the queue is completely full and dumping everything, WRED watches queue depth and starts randomly dropping packets when depth crosses a low threshold.

Weighted means the drop probability depends on the packet's priority (DSCP or IP Precedence). Lower-priority packets (higher drop precedence in AF terms) start being dropped at lower queue depths. Higher-priority packets are protected until queue depth is much deeper. This ensures voice and critical traffic survive congestion while bulk data takes the hit.

Example thresholds on a CBWFQ class queue: minimum threshold 20, maximum threshold 40, max drop probability 1/10. WRED behavior:

  • Queue depth < 20: no drops, all packets enqueued.
  • Queue depth 20-40: random drops, probability rises linearly from 0 to 1/10 based on depth.
  • Queue depth > 40: all packets dropped (tail drop fallback).

Different drop precedences within AF classes have different min/max thresholds. AF11 (low drop) might start dropping at queue depth 30; AF13 (high drop) at depth 15. This is how WRED enforces the drop precedence hierarchy.

Benefits: (1) TCP flows detect loss at staggered times — no global synchronization. (2) Queue depth stays below max, so voice and other priority traffic maintain low latency. (3) Low-priority traffic gets squeezed first, preserving mission-critical apps.

Voice in the LLQ is NOT subject to WRED. LLQ has its own policer and is managed separately. WRED applies to CBWFQ classes and the default class.

states: Queue 20-40: random drop, weighted by DSCP
Effect: queue rarely fills; voice protected

hack.memory: WRED = Weighted Random Early Detection. Start dropping packets BEFORE queue fills. 'Weighted' = low-priority dropped first. 'Early' = below tail-drop threshold. 'Random' = packets from different flows dropped at different times.
hack.practice: Draw a queue with thresholds: min, max, and 100%. Between min and max, show random drops of low-priority packets. Below min, no drops. Above max, tail drop. This diagram shows WRED's job in one picture.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what does WRED do?' = drop packets proactively to avoid tail drop. 'Why weighted?' = drop low-priority first.

4.7.e.info:
TCP global synchronization is the pathological pattern caused by tail drop on a congested link carrying many TCP flows. Every flow loses packets at the same time (when the queue fills), every sender backs off simultaneously, and then every sender ramps back up together. Throughput oscillates in waves instead of staying steady near link capacity.

The mechanism: TCP's congestion-control algorithm responds to packet loss by cutting its window size (typically halving it with classic Reno or TCP Cubic). When tail drop hits, every flow on the link sees loss in the same few milliseconds. They all halve their windows together. Total traffic suddenly drops to maybe 10-20% of link capacity. Senders then slowly ramp back up (slow start or congestion avoidance), and traffic grows until the queue fills again — and the cycle repeats.

Observable symptoms: utilization graphs that look like a sawtooth or waveform rather than a smooth line. Average utilization is surprisingly low (maybe 40-60%) on a link you thought was congested. Performance is inconsistent.

WRED breaks the synchronization by dropping packets at randomized times from different flows BEFORE the queue fills. Flow A loses a packet at t=1, flow B at t=2, flow C at t=3. Each flow backs off independently. Total traffic stays relatively steady at near-capacity. No waveform.

Also related: some modern TCP stacks (CUBIC, BBR) are less prone to synchronization, but the problem is still real on networks with many conventional TCP flows.

right.label: With WRED
items: Flows lose packets at staggered times

hack.memory: TCP global sync = everyone backs off at once because tail drop hits everyone at once. Bandwidth oscillates, sawtooth pattern, low average utilization. WRED fixes it by staggering drops across flows.
hack.practice: Think of a highway with traffic signals all timed together: everyone stops, everyone goes, throughput is terrible. Now stagger the signals: some always green, some red, but traffic keeps flowing. WRED = staggered signals for TCP.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what is TCP global synchronization?' = many TCP flows all back off simultaneously after tail drop. 'What mitigates it?' = WRED.

4.7.e.info:
Side-by-side comparison of the two congestion responses:

Tail drop — the naive default. Queue fills, everything past the end of the queue is dropped. Treats all packets equally at the point of failure. Causes TCP global synchronization. Available everywhere by default because it requires no configuration.

WRED — the intelligent response. Starts dropping packets randomly when the queue crosses a low threshold (below full). Drops lower-priority traffic first (weighted). Prevents the queue from filling, preserving latency for high-priority traffic. Breaks TCP global synchronization by staggering drops. Requires configuration (thresholds and max drop probability per DSCP or IPP class).

Decision matrix:

  • Reactive vs proactive: tail drop = reactive (drop when full). WRED = proactive (drop before full).
  • Fairness: tail drop = indiscriminate. WRED = class-aware, low priority dies first.
  • TCP behavior: tail drop = synchronization (waveform bandwidth). WRED = staggered (steady bandwidth).
  • Configuration: tail drop = none required, default. WRED = min/max thresholds per class.

Use case: always prefer WRED on any interface carrying mixed traffic classes or any link that experiences congestion. Tail drop is acceptable only as a last-resort fallback when WRED itself can't keep up (very rare with correct tuning).

In modern Cisco gear, WRED is often automatically enabled on CBWFQ queues. You just have to know the concept for CCNA.

hack.memory: Tail drop = reactive + indiscriminate. WRED = proactive + selective. Tail drop is the default on a raw queue; WRED is what you want. WRED prevents tail drop and TCP global sync in one mechanism.
hack.practice: Make a 4-row comparison table: (1) timing (reactive vs proactive), (2) selectivity (none vs weighted), (3) TCP impact (sync vs staggered), (4) config (none vs thresholds). This table covers every tail-drop-vs-WRED exam question.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Guaranteed exam question about the difference. Reactive/proactive + indiscriminate/selective is the frame that answers it cleanly.

4.7.f.info:
Policing enforces a strict traffic rate by immediately dropping or re-marking packets that exceed the configured limit. No buffering, no delay — excess traffic is dealt with instantly. Think of it as a hard ceiling with no tolerance for bursts.

Policing uses a token bucket model. The bucket fills with tokens at the committed information rate (CIR) up to the committed burst size (Bc). Each packet 'spends' tokens equal to its size.
