If tokens are available, the packet conforms and passes. If not, the packet exceeds and is either dropped or re-marked to a lower priority (typically AF13 or best-effort).

Three-color policer (commonly used): green (conforming) packets pass unchanged. Yellow (exceeding) packets are re-marked with higher drop precedence. Red (violating) packets are dropped. This lets you give some leniency to small bursts while still enforcing the hard ceiling for sustained excess.

Deployment points: policing can happen at ingress or egress. Ingress policing is typical at network boundaries — the customer's traffic is rate-limited as it enters the ISP's network. Egress policing is less common but used when you want to enforce a limit on traffic leaving a class.

Characteristics: bursty output (traffic is either passed at full rate or dropped), no added latency, no buffer memory required. Good for enforcing SLAs where bandwidth caps must be hard. Bad for TCP because drops trigger retransmissions; TCP will naturally adjust its sending rate but loses packets in the process.

hack.memory: Policing = immediate drop or re-mark of excess. Hard limit, no buffering. Token bucket (CIR + Bc). Ingress or egress. Bursty output. No added latency. Used at network boundaries for SLA enforcement.
hack.practice: Imagine an ISP limiting a customer to 50 Mbps. Customer sends 60 Mbps. Policer drops 10 Mbps instantly. Customer sees dropped packets, retransmits, TCP adjusts down to ~50 Mbps. The customer never exceeds 50 Mbps even briefly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what does policing do with excess traffic?' = drops or re-marks it. 'Can policing add latency?' = no, it's immediate.

4.7.f.info:
Shaping enforces a traffic rate by buffering excess packets and releasing them gradually when bandwidth becomes available. Unlike policing (which drops), shaping queues excess and transmits smoothly. The result is a steady output rate matching the shaped rate — but at the cost of added latency from buffering.

Shaping also uses a token bucket, but instead of dropping when tokens run out, excess packets are held in a shape queue. When new tokens arrive, queued packets are sent. The queue grows during bursts and drains during lulls.

Egress only: shaping is applied on outbound interfaces only. You can't shape traffic coming into a device — that requires policing (drop) or backpressure signaling. Shaping makes sense on egress because you control the buffer and the send timing of traffic leaving your device.

Use case: typical on WAN egress where the physical link speed is higher than the contracted/available rate. Example: a 100 Mbps Ethernet link to an ISP with a 50 Mbps Committed Information Rate. The router shapes outbound traffic to 50 Mbps, smoothing bursts, so the provider never sees excess (which they would drop with their own policer).

Characteristics: smooth output (no bursts beyond shaped rate), adds latency (packets wait in shape queue), uses buffer memory, good for TCP (no drops, no retransmits), bad for voice (buffering = latency).

Design rule: don't shape voice. Voice needs low latency; any buffering hurts. Voice should go through an LLQ (strict priority) and avoid the shaping queue.

states: Output stream stays at shaped rate

hack.memory: Shaping = buffer excess, release gradually. Smooth output, adds latency. Egress only. Good for TCP (no drops). Bad for voice (latency). Typical on WAN links where physical speed > contracted rate.
hack.practice: A 100 Mbps link shaped to 50 Mbps: when the device tries to send 80 Mbps, 50 Mbps goes out now, 30 Mbps waits in the shape buffer and goes out in the next slot. Output graph looks like a flat line at 50, not a bursty 80.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'what does shaping do with excess?' = buffer it. 'Where is shaping applied?' = egress only. 'Effect on latency?' = adds latency.

4.7.f.info:
The most-tested QoS comparison on the CCNA. Memorize every row:

Excess traffic: Policing DROPS (or re-marks). Shaping BUFFERS (queues and delays).

Deployment: Policing = ingress OR egress. Shaping = egress ONLY.

Output pattern: Policing = bursty (packets either pass at full rate or get dropped, no smoothing). Shaping = smooth (steady rate at the shaped cap).

Latency impact: Policing adds no latency (immediate action). Shaping adds latency (buffering).

TCP friendliness: Policing causes TCP retransmits (drops trigger loss detection). Shaping is TCP-friendly (no drops from shaping itself).

Voice friendliness: Policing is OK for voice (no buffering, losses happen fast and TCP doesn't care because voice uses UDP). Shaping is BAD for voice (any buffering = latency, voice hates latency).

Typical use case: Policing at network boundaries for SLA enforcement (ISP limits customer to CIR). Shaping on WAN egress when the physical link exceeds the contracted rate (router shapes to contracted rate so ISP doesn't drop excess).

One-liner to remember: 'Policing is a cliff. Shaping is a ramp.' Policing drops the excess over the edge instantly. Shaping eases the excess down gradually.

hack.memory: Policing = drop (cliff). Shaping = buffer (ramp). Policing = ingress or egress. Shaping = egress only. Policing = no latency. Shaping = adds latency. Don't shape voice.
hack.practice: Write this 6-row table on paper without looking. Do it 3 times in a row. If you can reproduce it cold, you'll nail every policing-vs-shaping exam question.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Literally the most tested QoS comparison. The 6-row table covers every variant.

4.7.g.info:
Shaping's core tradeoff: it adds latency because excess traffic sits in a buffer waiting for bandwidth. The shape queue is exactly that — a queue. Packets wait their turn. Wait = latency.

How much latency? Depends on queue depth and shaped rate. A shape queue holding 100 ms of traffic adds 100 ms of latency to packets at the tail. On a voice call with a 150 ms one-way budget, a 100 ms shape queue wipes out 67% of your latency budget before the packet even leaves the router.

This is why voice should never be shaped. Voice should go through an LLQ (strict priority) where it bypasses the shape queue entirely. Shape the bulk and default traffic; let voice fly straight through.

Shaping is acceptable for: bulk data (backups, file transfers — no latency sensitivity), transactional data (some tolerance for latency), video streaming with large jitter buffers (can handle variable latency). Shaping is NOT acceptable for: voice, real-time video conferencing, financial trading, interactive terminal sessions.

Design rule: combine shaping with LLQ. The policy-map uses shape average 50000000 on the parent class to cap the interface to 50 Mbps, while inside the shape, an LLQ provides strict priority for voice. Voice exits via the LLQ without shape-queue delay; everything else is shaped smoothly.

On WAN links where the provider drops excess, shaping makes sense. Better to smooth out bursts on your side than have the provider drop packets on theirs. But always protect voice from the shape queue.

hack.memory: Shaping = latency. Never shape voice — it hates latency. Voice uses LLQ to bypass the shape queue. Everything else can be shaped.
hack.practice: Calculate: shape queue holds 80 ms of traffic. Voice budget is 150 ms end-to-end. A shaped voice packet immediately loses 53% of its latency budget. This math explains why voice skips the shape queue via LLQ.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'why is shaping bad for voice?' = buffering adds latency. 'How do you protect voice when shaping?' = put voice in an LLQ that bypasses the shape queue.

4.7.g.info:
Policing's compensating advantage over shaping: it adds zero latency. The policer's decision is instantaneous — conform or exceed, pass or drop. No queue, no wait. Packets that conform pass at line rate. Packets that exceed are dropped immediately.

This makes policing useful when latency must not increase and you can tolerate drops. Voice is actually a reasonable fit for policing in some scenarios: voice uses UDP (no retransmit overhead), voice codecs handle small amounts of loss gracefully (≤1% is generally OK), and voice hates latency more than it hates loss. Better to drop a voice packet than delay it.

Policing at the ingress of a priority queue is the classic pattern: priority percent 33 in an LLQ creates a built-in policer. Voice packets above the cap are dropped, not buffered. Voice latency stays low; excess voice (which shouldn't happen in a well-designed network) is sacrificed rather than delayed.

Compare to shaping, which would buffer the excess voice and add latency — much worse for voice quality.

Policing is also appropriate for enforcing strict SLAs. An ISP selling a 50 Mbps circuit will policer at 50 Mbps. The customer either sends ≤ 50 Mbps (conforms) or gets dropped above that. TCP adjusts; UDP users notice drops. Either way, the ISP enforces their product boundary.

Downside: policing's bursty output is hard on TCP (many flows retransmitting creates congestion churn) and causes visible packet loss to users. If TCP-friendliness and smoothness matter more than latency, shape instead.

hack.memory: Policing = zero latency (drop or pass instantly). Good for voice at the edge when you can tolerate small losses. Shaping = adds latency (buffers). Bad for voice.
hack.practice: LLQ with priority + built-in policer = voice gets strict priority up to its cap, then excess is dropped (not queued). This is exactly why LLQ uses policing, not shaping, for the priority cap.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Exam: 'does policing add latency?' = no. 'Is policing OK for voice?' = yes, better than shaping because no buffering.

4.7.g.info:
When to choose policing vs shaping in the real world:

Policing use cases:

  • ISP edge SLA enforcement — customer gets rate-limited at ingress. Drop excess, enforce the product.
  • LLQ priority cap — prevent voice from starving other queues. Built-in policer drops voice above cap.
  • Security rate limits — limit SYN/ICMP rate to prevent abuse. Drops attackers.
  • Service tier enforcement — 'bronze' traffic gets policed to 10 Mbps, 'gold' to 50 Mbps, strict caps.

Shaping use cases:

  • WAN egress smoothing — link speed exceeds contracted rate; shape to contracted rate so provider never drops.
  • Bursty app management — smooth out large database syncs or backup flows.
  • Frame Relay or MPLS sub-rate circuits — physical port faster than CIR, shape to CIR.
  • TCP-heavy traffic — prefer shaping over policing for bulk TCP to avoid retransmit churn.

Hybrid designs are common: shape the WAN egress interface overall (e.g., shape average to the provider CIR), and within the shape, run an LLQ + CBWFQ hierarchy. LLQ's built-in policer caps voice. CBWFQ reserves bandwidth per data class. Shape queue smooths overall output. Three QoS tools working together.

CCNA won't ask you to design this — it asks when each tool is the right choice. Policing = hard caps, no latency, ingress or egress. Shaping = smoothing, added latency, egress only.

hack.memory: Policing = enforcement (hard cap, drops). Shaping = smoothing (soft cap, buffers). Use police at the ingress boundary; use shape on WAN egress. Combine them in hierarchical policies with LLQ + CBWFQ.
hack.practice: Given a scenario: '100 Mbps port, ISP contracted for 50 Mbps, provider polices excess.' Your side should SHAPE to 50 so ISP doesn't drop. Given: 'customer purchased a 20 Mbps plan.' ISP should POLICE to 20 to enforce the SLA. Each scenario has one correct tool.
hack.meta: Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 12. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 46. Exam: match the use case to the right tool. Memorize the policing-vs-shaping table and the typical deployment patterns.

4.7.h.info:
The QoS trust boundary is the point in the network where QoS markings (DSCP/CoS) on incoming traffic are first accepted as valid. Before the trust boundary, endpoint-supplied markings are suspect and typically re-marked or reset. After the trust boundary, every device trusts the marking and applies the matching PHB.

Why it matters: without a trust boundary, any endpoint can mark its own traffic as high-priority. A user could configure their PC to tag all its traffic as EF (DSCP 46). The network, believing it's voice, would put it in the strict-priority LLQ and starve legitimate voice calls. Trust boundaries prevent this abuse.

Typical placement: the access layer switch — the first network device the endpoint connects to. The switch either trusts specific devices on specific ports (IP phones) or re-marks everything from untrusted devices to a safe default (usually DSCP 0).

Modern deployments use Cisco's AutoQoS or similar tools to auto-configure trust boundaries. AutoQoS on a switch port that recognizes a CDP/LLDP handshake with an IP phone will trust the phone's CoS markings automatically. Other ports default to untrusted / re-mark-to-0.

Beyond the trust boundary, all devices inside the enterprise trust the markings. Core and distribution routers don't re-classify or re-mark — they simply read DSCP and apply the PHB. This is efficient because classification (especially NBAR) is CPU-expensive; doing it once at the edge is cheaper than doing it at every hop.

params.threat: Untrusted endpoint marking its own traffic as high-priority

hack.memory: Trust boundary = where QoS markings start being believed. Typically the access switch. Before the boundary, markings are suspect and often re-written. After the boundary, every device trusts the mark.
hack.practice: Sketch: PC → IP Phone → Access Switch → Distribution → Core. Draw the trust boundary at the access switch. Phone is trusted (CoS preserved). PC is untrusted (re-marked). Everything past the access switch trusts DSCP and applies PHB.

4.7.h.info:
Design rule for trust boundaries: place them as close to the traffic source as possible. In practice, this means the access-layer switch (or the IP phone connected to it).

Why close to the source? Two reasons. First, shorter trust chains = fewer places to compromise. The fewer hops between the endpoint and the trust boundary, the less attack surface for someone to spoof markings. Second, classification at the source has the most context: the device knows who's sending, from which port, with what CDP/LLDP identity. Distribution switches and core routers don't have that context — they'd have to infer from IPs and ports, which is less accurate.

Typical boundary positions:

  • IP phone — the phone marks its own RTP voice as EF/CoS=5 and signals (SIP/SCCP) as CS3. Phone is the trust boundary for voice.
  • Access switch — trusts the IP phone (CoS preserved for voice VLAN), does not trust the PC (re-marks PC traffic to 0 or classifies via NBAR/ACLs).
  • Dedicated server VLAN access switch — trusts known server traffic because the servers are managed and trusted. Re-marks external-facing traffic.

Beyond the access layer, every device trusts DSCP. Distribution and core switches/routers apply PHB based on the marking — no re-classification. This keeps the network efficient: expensive classification happens once, at the edge.

If you can't trust the IP phone (rare — usually phones are trusted), the access switch becomes the first trusted device. It classifies voice traffic by port (5060, 16384-32767) or by ACL matching the phone's subnet, then marks DSCP. The phone is then untrusted and re-marked.

4.7.h.info:
Without a trust boundary, any endpoint can mark its own traffic as high-priority DSCP. A single malicious or ignorant PC marking all traffic as EF (46) can take over the priority queue, starve real voice traffic, and collapse call quality for the entire enterprise.

Consider the attack: user installs a script on their PC that sets the DSCP field on outgoing packets to 46. Network sees DSCP 46 and puts it in the LLQ. LLQ services it first, ahead of legitimate voice. If the PC sends enough traffic, voice latency spikes, jitter explodes, calls drop. All because one endpoint successfully 'claimed' priority without being authorized.

Trust boundary prevents this. At the access switch, the PC's traffic is untrusted and its DSCP is reset to 0. Now the PC's traffic gets best-effort treatment no matter what it claims. Voice from the IP phone (which the switch trusts) retains its EF marking and its priority queue slot.

Real-world implications:

  • Enterprise networks with VoIP MUST have trust boundaries; otherwise a single misbehaving endpoint can degrade every call in the building.
  • Service providers configure ingress policing/remarking on customer-facing links so customers can't cheat their way into better QoS classes.
  • Even accidental misconfigurations (a sysadmin tagging test traffic wrong) are contained by trust boundaries.

The trust boundary is a security control as much as a QoS design element. Treat it like an authentication point: 'is this marking legitimate? who marked it?'. If you can't verify, don't trust.

states: Without boundary: trusted → LLQ → starves real voice
With boundary: re-marked to 0 → default queue
